_meta.authoringNotes: Umbrella page covering EKC (adenovirus), VKC, AKC, sicca, herpetic, phlyctenular, and Theodore subtypes. Cross-checked against AAO PPP Cornea/External Disease 2023, ACAAI VKC/AKC reviews, Kaufman 2011 PMID 21956976, BenEzra VKC reviews.

h1Title: Keratoconjunctivitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Keratoconjunctivitis is concurrent inflammation of the cornea and conjunctiva, an umbrella diagnosis covering seven distinct subtypes with shared surface findings but very different causes and treatments. The most contagious form, epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC), is driven by adenovirus serotypes 8, 19, and 37 and causes outbreaks in schools, clinics, and military barracks. Vernal keratoconjunctivitis (VKC) is a severe allergic form affecting boys aged 5-25 in hot dry climates, while atopic keratoconjunctivitis (AKC) accompanies adult atopic dermatitis. Herpetic disease presents with a pathognomonic dendritic ulcer. Symptoms overlap: red eye, foreign-body sensation, photophobia, and watery or mucoid discharge. Untreated, corneal involvement can scar and reduce vision; correct subtyping decides whether antivirals, steroids, mast-cell stabilizers, or only supportive care are appropriate.

keyStats.prevalence: EKC accounts for 15-70% of acute viral conjunctivitis outbreaks worldwide; VKC affects up to 0.4% of children in Mediterranean and South Asian regions
keyStats.demographics: EKC affects all ages; VKC predominantly boys 5-25 (male:female 4:1); AKC adults 30-50 with atopic dermatitis; HSV keratitis 60,000 new US cases/year
keyStats.avgAge: EKC any age; VKC onset 5-15 years; AKC 30-50; herpetic 20-40 primary, recurrent throughout life
keyStats.globalCases: Adenoviral conjunctivitis estimated at tens of millions globally each year; HSV keratitis leading infectious cause of corneal blindness worldwide

definition: Keratoconjunctivitis (ICD-10: H16.20) refers to simultaneous inflammation of the cornea (keratitis) and conjunctiva (conjunctivitis), distinct from isolated conjunctivitis because corneal involvement carries a higher risk of visual loss through scarring, neovascularization, or ulceration. It is not a single disease but a clinical umbrella encompassing infectious, allergic, autoimmune, and dry-eye etiologies. Subtypes share surface findings of conjunctival hyperemia, papillary or follicular response, and corneal staining, but differ in mechanism: adenoviral EKC produces follicular conjunctivitis with subepithelial corneal infiltrates appearing 7-14 days after onset; vernal disease is a giant papillary IgE/Th2-mediated allergic reaction with palpebral cobblestone papillae or limbal Horner-Trantas dots; atopic disease overlaps with eczema; sicca (dry-eye) keratoconjunctivitis reflects aqueous or evaporative tear deficiency often in Sjogren syndrome; herpetic disease is HSV-1 reactivation producing a dendritic epithelial ulcer; phlyctenular disease is a hypersensitivity nodule overlying tuberculin or staphylococcal antigen; and limbic keratoconjunctivitis of Theodore is an autoimmune superior-limbus inflammation with rose-bengal staining. Diagnosis and management belong to ophthalmology, frequently subspecialty cornea/external disease.

typesClassification.description: Adenovirus serotypes 8, 19, and 37 are the most virulent. Highly contagious through fomites, droplets, and contaminated tonometer tips. Causes outbreaks in schools and clinics. Subepithelial corneal infiltrates emerge 7-14 days after symptom onset and can persist for months, reducing vision.

Severe seasonal or perennial allergic disease most common in boys aged 5-25 in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and South Asian climates. Two forms: palpebral with giant cobblestone papillae on the upper tarsus, and limbal with Horner-Trantas dots (clusters of eosinophils at the limbus). Resolves around puberty in most patients.

Chronic bilateral disease in adults with atopic dermatitis. Lids show eczematous changes and chronic blepharitis; tarsal conjunctiva develops fibrosis. Corneal complications include persistent epithelial defects and keratoconus. Persists beyond puberty unlike VKC.

Aqueous-deficient or evaporative tear-film instability producing punctate corneal staining and conjunctival irritation. Severe forms are linked to Sjogren syndrome, graft-versus-host disease, and Stevens-Johnson syndrome.

HSV-1 primary infection or reactivation. Pathognomonic dendritic epithelial ulcer with terminal bulbs stains brightly with fluorescein and rose bengal. Stromal disease and endotheliitis can follow recurrences. Corneal sensation is reduced over the dendrite.

A type-IV delayed hypersensitivity nodule (phlyctenule) at the limbus or cornea in response to bacterial antigens, classically Mycobacterium tuberculosis (more common in endemic regions) and Staphylococcus aureus.

Recurrent inflammation of the superior bulbar conjunctiva and limbus with rose-bengal staining of the upper limbus. Associated with thyroid dysfunction in roughly 30% of cases. Typically affects middle-aged women.

primarySymptoms: Bilateral or unilateral red eye with diffuse conjunctival injection, often starting in one eye and spreading to the other within 2-7 days in viral forms.
Foreign-body or gritty sensation behind the eyelid, worse in the morning and with blinking, present in nearly all subtypes regardless of cause.
Photophobia (light sensitivity), which becomes severe once corneal subepithelial infiltrates or epithelial defects develop.
Watery, serous discharge in viral EKC versus thick stringy mucoid discharge in vernal and atopic forms, helping distinguish etiology at the bedside.
Eyelid swelling and a palpable pre-auricular lymph node on the affected side, classic for EKC and HSV but absent in allergic disease.
Itching that dominates the symptom picture in vernal and atopic forms, often described by children as a need to rub the eyes constantly.
Blurred or reduced vision when subepithelial infiltrates, dendritic ulcers, or shield ulcers form on the central cornea.
Pain ranging from mild irritation in viral and allergic disease to sharp stabbing pain in herpetic dendritic keratitis.
Pseudomembranes or true membranes on the tarsal conjunctiva in severe EKC, requiring gentle peeling at the slit lamp.
Recurrent flares on a defined schedule (vernal season, post-cold reactivation of HSV) that help establish chronicity and subtype.
earlyWarningSigns: One-sided red watery eye in a household where another member has had pinkeye in the last 14 days
Severe itching plus thick stringy mucus during pollen season in a school-age boy
Sharp unilateral eye pain with watering and reduced corneal sensation following a cold sore or upper respiratory infection
Persistent gritty sensation and morning eyelid sticking in a patient with rheumatoid arthritis or known Sjogren syndrome
Recurrent superior-eye irritation that worsens with blinking in a middle-aged woman with thyroid disease
emergencySigns: Sudden vision loss with intense pain and corneal whitening — bacterial or fungal corneal ulcer must be excluded same day
Severe pain after a steroid eye drop in suspected herpetic disease — steroids alone worsen HSV epithelial ulcers and can perforate the cornea
Vesicular rash following the trigeminal V1 distribution with eye involvement (Hutchinson sign) — herpes zoster ophthalmicus needs urgent oral antiviral within 72 hours
Hyperacute purulent discharge in a sexually active adult — gonococcal conjunctivitis can perforate the cornea within 24-48 hours
Photophobia with worsening vision 7-14 days after a flu-like illness in a contact lens wearer — secondary microbial keratitis on EKC infiltrates

causes.description: Serotypes 8, 19, and 37 are the most virulent and produce the classic EKC syndrome. Spread occurs through direct contact, contaminated towels and instruments, and respiratory droplets. The virus survives on dry surfaces for up to 30 days, fueling clinic and school outbreaks.

Seasonal allergen exposure in genetically predisposed individuals triggers mast-cell degranulation, eosinophil infiltration, and chronic Th2 cytokine release (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13). This drives the giant papillary reaction, Horner-Trantas dots, and eventual shield ulcer formation.

HSV-1 establishes latency in the trigeminal ganglion after primary infection. Reactivation along the ophthalmic branch produces a dendritic epithelial ulcer. Triggers include UV exposure, fever, immunosuppression, and ocular surgery.

Either aqueous deficiency (Sjogren syndrome, age-related lacrimal gland atrophy, post-radiation) or evaporative loss (meibomian gland dysfunction, blepharitis) produces an unstable tear film that fails to protect the corneal and conjunctival epithelium.

causes.cause: Type IV hypersensitivity to bacterial antigens (phlyctenular)
causes.description: A delayed cell-mediated reaction to staphylococcal cell-wall antigens or tuberculoprotein produces a small inflammatory nodule at the limbus or cornea. Latent tuberculosis is a recognized driver in endemic regions.

causes.cause: Autoimmune surface inflammation (Theodore and dry-eye associated)
causes.description: Localized autoimmunity at the superior bulbar conjunctiva, frequently linked to thyroid dysfunction, drives the recurrent picture of superior limbic disease. Generalized autoimmunity in Sjogren or graft-versus-host disease drives the sicca subtype.

riskFactors.factor: Recent exposure to a person with pinkeye or to a healthcare setting with an outbreak
riskFactors.description: Adenovirus transmits efficiently via fomites; secondary attack rates of 10-50% are reported in household and clinic contacts.

riskFactors.factor: Atopic family history (asthma, eczema, allergic rhinitis)
riskFactors.description: Personal or first-degree relative atopy raises the risk of VKC and AKC substantially. Up to 75% of VKC patients have at least one other atopic disease.
Vernal disease shows a 4:1 male predominance and almost always resolves by the mid-twenties. Hormonal modulation of the allergic response is suspected but unproven.
Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, North African, and South Asian regions report the highest VKC prevalence. UV exposure and airborne allergens both drive disease activity.
Adults with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis have a 25-40% lifetime risk of AKC. Periocular eczema and chronic eye-rubbing both contribute.
Contact lenses disrupt the tear film, harbor microbes, and amplify any corneal insult — raising the risk of secondary microbial keratitis on top of viral or allergic disease.

riskFactors.factor: Sjogren syndrome and other autoimmune disorders
riskFactors.description: Autoimmune lacrimal gland destruction is the leading cause of severe aqueous-deficient sicca keratoconjunctivitis. Rheumatoid arthritis and systemic lupus contribute similarly.

riskFactors.factor: Prior HSV ocular disease or oral cold sores
riskFactors.description: Once HSV-1 reaches the trigeminal ganglion, lifelong reactivation risk persists. Recurrence rate after a first ocular episode is 25% at one year and 50% at ten years.
Roughly 30% of patients with superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis of Theodore have hypo- or hyperthyroidism; correcting thyroid status often improves the eye disease.

riskFactors.factor: Crowded living conditions and shared towels
riskFactors.description: Boarding schools, military barracks, refugee camps, and densely staffed clinics consistently report the highest EKC attack rates.

affectedDemographics: EKC outbreaks recur globally with no clear sex preference; healthcare workers and military recruits are at elevated risk
VKC clusters in the equatorial belt: India, Pakistan, North Africa, Mediterranean Europe; prevalence reaches 4 per 1,000 in some studies
AKC affects 25-40% of adults with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis
HSV keratitis is the leading infectious cause of corneal blindness in high-income countries, with 60,000 new US cases annually and 1.5 million worldwide
Theodore disease typically affects women aged 40-60, with a strong association with thyroid disease
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis hinges on slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist who can distinguish the seven subtypes by the pattern of inflammation. The clinician looks for the type of papillary response (giant cobblestone papillae of VKC, fine papillae of allergy, follicles of viral disease), the location and pattern of corneal staining with fluorescein and rose bengal, the presence of subepithelial infiltrates pathognomonic for EKC, a dendritic ulcer for HSV, Horner-Trantas dots at the limbus for VKC, or rose-bengal staining of the superior limbus for Theodore disease. Palpation reveals tender pre-auricular lymphadenopathy in EKC and primary HSV but not in allergic disease. Point-of-care testing now plays a defined role: the RPS Adeno Detector rapid antigen test has 88% sensitivity and 91% specificity for adenovirus and confirms EKC within 10 minutes. HSV PCR of corneal scrapings approaches 100% specificity. Conjunctival scraping with Giemsa staining shows eosinophils in allergic disease and intranuclear inclusions in HSV. For sicca disease, tear break-up time below 10 seconds and Schirmer test below 5 mm at 5 minutes confirm aqueous deficiency. Cultures are reserved for atypical or treatment-refractory cases. Critical differentials include bacterial conjunctivitis (purulent discharge, papillary not follicular response), bacterial keratitis (hypopyon, dense infiltrate), and herpes zoster ophthalmicus (V1 dermatomal rash, Hutchinson sign).

diagnosticTests.test: Slit-lamp biomicroscopy with fluorescein and rose bengal staining
diagnosticTests.purpose: The decisive test. Identifies dendritic ulcers, subepithelial infiltrates, Horner-Trantas dots, papillae versus follicles, and the punctate staining patterns specific to each subtype.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient sits at a microscope; the clinician instills anesthetic and dye drops; examination takes 5-10 minutes. Bright light is briefly uncomfortable. No needles.

diagnosticTests.test: Adenovirus rapid antigen test (RPS Adeno Detector)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms or excludes adenoviral etiology in suspected EKC within 10 minutes. Sensitivity 88%, specificity 91%. Useful for outbreak control and to justify infection-control isolation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab is gently wiped along the lower conjunctival sac after topical anesthetic. The result reads like a pregnancy test cassette in 10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects HSV DNA in suspected herpetic disease, particularly when the dendrite is atypical or has been treated with steroids. Sensitivity 95-100%, specificity near 100%.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After anesthetic drops, a thin cotton or dacron swab is rubbed across the suspected lesion. Results return in 24-72 hours from most molecular labs.

diagnosticTests.test: Conjunctival scraping with Giemsa or Wright stain
diagnosticTests.purpose: Cell-type analysis distinguishes etiologies: predominance of eosinophils in vernal and atopic disease, lymphocytes and monocytes in viral disease, neutrophils in bacterial disease, multinucleated giant cells in herpetic disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After anesthetic, the lower conjunctival surface is scraped with a sterile spatula; cells are smeared on a slide. The procedure takes 2-3 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Tear break-up time (TBUT) and Schirmer test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantify tear-film stability and aqueous production in suspected sicca disease. TBUT below 10 seconds and Schirmer below 5 mm at 5 minutes indicate clinically significant dry eye.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fluorescein is instilled and the patient blinks; the clinician times tear-film break-up. For Schirmer, a paper strip is hooked over the lower lid and tear migration is measured at 5 minutes. Mild stinging is common.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for atypical, severe, or treatment-refractory cases where superinfection or alternative diagnoses are suspected. Helps distinguish from microbial keratitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Corneal or conjunctival scrapings are plated on blood, chocolate, and Sabouraud agar. Preliminary results at 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Thyroid function tests in suspected Theodore disease
diagnosticTests.purpose: TSH, free T4, and thyroid antibodies are checked because approximately 30% of patients with superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis have hypo- or hyperthyroidism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results return same day in most labs.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is dictated by subtype and runs across four very different tracks. Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis is mainly supportive: cool compresses, preservative-free lubricants, strict hand-washing, and isolation from work or school for 10-14 days while the patient remains shedding virus. Topical steroids reduce immediate inflammation and the cosmetic impact of subepithelial infiltrates but can prolong viral shedding, and AAO Preferred Practice Patterns recommend using them only when infiltrates threaten visual acuity. Povidone-iodine 5% irrigation and topical cidofovir or ganciclovir gel remain investigational with limited evidence. Vernal keratoconjunctivitis is treated with topical antihistamine/mast-cell stabilizers (olopatadine 0.1-0.2% twice daily, ketotifen 0.025% twice daily) as first-line, with topical cyclosporine 0.05-2% or tacrolimus 0.03% ointment for steroid-sparing maintenance; short pulses of topical loteprednol or fluorometholone are used for severe flares. Allergen avoidance, cool compresses, and lid hygiene support medical therapy. Atopic keratoconjunctivitis usually requires systemic immunomodulation — dupilumab is now first-line for moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis and improves ocular symptoms, while topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment manages periocular eczema. Herpetic disease responds to topical trifluridine 1% nine times daily or oral acyclovir 400 mg five times daily for 7-10 days for epithelial disease; stromal disease and endotheliitis additionally require topical steroids with antiviral cover, and chronic suppressive oral acyclovir 400 mg twice daily for one year halves recurrence rates (HEDS trial). Phlyctenular disease responds rapidly to topical steroids plus a cycloplegic; underlying tuberculosis must be treated when present. Sicca disease management is detailed in the dedicated dry-eye and Sjogren syndrome pages.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical olopatadine 0.1-0.2% (one drop twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Combined H1 antihistamine and mast-cell stabilizer. First-line for vernal and seasonal allergic keratoconjunctivitis. Onset within 30 minutes; well-tolerated long-term.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 75-85% of allergic patients within 1-2 weeks of consistent use.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical cyclosporine 0.05% to 2% (twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitor that reduces T-cell-mediated inflammation. Steroid-sparing maintenance therapy for moderate-to-severe VKC, AKC, and severe dry-eye. Stinging on instillation is the main side effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant reduction in papillary inflammation in 70-80% of refractory VKC patients at 3 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical loteprednol 0.5% or fluorometholone 0.1% (four times daily, tapered)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-pulse topical steroids for severe allergic or post-viral inflammation. Surface steroids carry risks of cataract, intraocular pressure rise, and infection — use limited to 2-4 weeks under ophthalmology supervision.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid symptom control in 80-90% of severe flares within 3-5 days.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical trifluridine 1% (one drop nine times daily for 10-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line topical antiviral for HSV epithelial keratitis. Active against the replicating virus but with surface toxicity that limits use beyond 14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Healing of dendritic ulcers in 85-95% of patients within 7-14 days.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral acyclovir 400 mg five times daily for 7-10 days (epithelial) or twice daily for chronic suppression
medicalTreatments.description: Systemic antiviral for HSV epithelial keratitis as an alternative to topical antivirals; also for stromal disease and endotheliitis with topical steroid cover. Long-term suppression halves recurrence rates per the HEDS trial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Epithelial healing comparable to topical trifluridine; suppression cuts recurrence from 32% to 19% over 12 months (HEDS).

medicalTreatments.name: Topical tacrolimus 0.03-0.1% ointment (once or twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitor used off-label on the eyelid skin for AKC and on the ocular surface for refractory VKC. Particularly useful for periocular eczema and as a steroid-sparing agent.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom and sign improvement in 75-85% of refractory AKC and VKC patients at 8 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Dupilumab 300 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: IL-4/IL-13 receptor antagonist for moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis. Improves ocular AKC symptoms in parallel with skin disease, though a subset develop dupilumab-associated conjunctivitis requiring co-management with ophthalmology.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Atopic dermatitis EASI-75 response in 50-60% of patients at 16 weeks; ocular AKC symptom improvement parallels skin response in most.

medicalTreatments.name: Preservative-free artificial tears and cool compresses
medicalTreatments.description: Universal supportive care for all subtypes. Lubricants dilute inflammatory mediators, restore tear-film stability, and provide symptomatic relief. Cool compresses constrict vessels and reduce itch and swelling.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 60-80% of mild-to-moderate cases as monotherapy; adjunctive in all severe cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Pseudomembrane and membrane peeling at the slit lamp
surgicalOptions.description: True or pseudomembranes complicating severe EKC are gently peeled with forceps after anesthetic drops. Prevents symblepharon (lid-to-globe adhesions) and accelerates resolution.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective in nearly all selected cases; recurrence of membranes is common and may require repeat peeling every 2-3 days.

surgicalOptions.name: Amniotic membrane transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: For severe non-healing epithelial defects, shield ulcers in VKC, or chemical-burn-like sicca disease. The amniotic membrane provides anti-inflammatory and pro-healing signals.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Epithelial healing in 80-90% of severe persistent epithelial defects within 2-4 weeks.

surgicalOptions.name: Penetrating or lamellar keratoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Corneal transplant reserved for end-stage scarring, perforation, or visually disabling subepithelial infiltrates that do not clear with topical steroids over 1-2 years.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year graft survival 70-90% in non-inflammatory indications; lower in active surface disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Lid hygiene with warm compresses and tea-tree-oil wipes
alternativeTreatments.description: Evidence-based adjunct for blepharitis-associated keratoconjunctivitis and meibomian gland dysfunction. Demodex-associated blepharitis responds to tea-tree-oil-based products in randomized trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 supplementation (EPA/DHA 1-2 g daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Mixed evidence from the DREAM trial and meta-analyses for dry-eye keratoconjunctivitis. Modest symptom improvement at 3 months in some patients; reasonable adjunct, not a substitute for tear replacement.

whySeeSpecialist: An ophthalmologist should evaluate any red eye with reduced vision, photophobia, foreign-body sensation that persists beyond 7 days, suspected dendritic ulcer, contact lens wearer with corneal involvement, or recurrent allergic disease that disrupts schooling. Subspecialty cornea/external disease input is appropriate for refractory VKC, AKC, recurrent HSV, severe sicca disease, and any patient considering systemic immunomodulators.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in ophthalmology with cornea/external disease fellowship for complex cases. The clinician should have slit-lamp expertise, access to corneal cultures and PCR, and familiarity with topical immunomodulators and steroid-sparing protocols. Continuity matters — surveillance for steroid-induced cataract and intraocular pressure rise is essential in long-term VKC and AKC care.
preventionStrategies: Wash hands thoroughly and avoid touching the eyes during local pinkeye outbreaks to interrupt adenovirus transmission
Disinfect contact lenses according to manufacturer instructions and avoid swimming or showering in lenses
Identify and avoid known seasonal allergens, use HEPA filters indoors, and keep windows shut on high-pollen days for VKC patients
Apply UV-protective sunglasses outdoors, particularly in equatorial regions, to reduce VKC flares and HSV reactivation
Maintain meticulous lid hygiene for adults with atopic dermatitis to prevent flares of AKC
Consider long-term oral acyclovir suppression after a first HSV recurrence, especially in graft recipients or those with stromal disease
lifestyleModifications: Replace mascara, eye drops, and cosmetics during and after any infectious episode to prevent re-inoculation
Use cool compresses 2-3 times daily during active flares to reduce itch, swelling, and inflammatory burden
Avoid rubbing the eyes — mechanical trauma drives both VKC papillary hypertrophy and keratoconus in atopic patients
Adjust ambient humidity (40-60%) and avoid direct air-conditioning drafts to support tear-film stability
Take screen breaks every 20 minutes (20-20-20 rule) to reduce evaporative dry-eye load on the ocular surface
Communicate ocular symptoms early to dermatologists managing systemic atopic disease so that ocular flares are not overlooked

recommended: Oily fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) for omega-3 fatty acids supporting tear-film stability
Leafy greens and orange vegetables high in lutein, zeaxanthin, and vitamin A for ocular surface health
Adequate water intake of 1.5-2 liters daily to prevent dehydration-related tear deficiency
Probiotic-rich foods to support the immune response in patients with atopic disease
Vitamin-D-replete diet (sun, fortified dairy, fatty fish) given lower vitamin D levels in chronic dry-eye
avoid: Known food allergens in atopic patients (egg, dairy, peanut where personally identified)
Excess alcohol, which worsens evaporative dry eye and disrupts sleep-related corneal repair
High-sugar diets, which promote inflammation and meibomian gland dysfunction
Excess caffeine if it contributes to systemic dehydration in dry-eye-prone individuals
Smoking and second-hand smoke, which worsen every subtype of keratoconjunctivitis

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is safe and beneficial. During an active infectious flare (EKC, HSV), avoid swimming pools, shared towels, and contact sports for 10-14 days. Sunglasses are recommended for outdoor exercise during VKC flares to limit UV and allergen exposure.
dailyManagement: Instill preservative-free artificial tears 4-6 times daily during active disease and 2-3 times daily for maintenance
Use prescribed antihistamine/mast-cell stabilizer drops consistently through the allergy season, not only when symptomatic
Apply cool compresses for 10 minutes 2-3 times daily during flares of allergic disease
Keep a single set of personal towels and washcloths during infectious episodes to prevent household spread
Track flare patterns, triggers, and treatment response in a brief diary to share with the ophthalmologist
prognosis: Prognosis depends entirely on subtype. EKC is self-limited and most patients fully recover within 2-3 weeks, although subepithelial corneal infiltrates may persist for months and reduce vision; long-term sequelae are uncommon with appropriate care. VKC resolves spontaneously around puberty in over 95% of patients; the main long-term concerns are steroid-induced cataract, glaucoma, and shield ulcers. AKC follows the course of underlying atopic dermatitis and can produce permanent corneal scarring, keratoconus, and cataract in 5-10% of severe cases. HSV recurs in 25% of patients at one year and 50% at ten years after the first episode; chronic oral acyclovir suppression halves this rate. Phlyctenular disease resolves over weeks with topical steroid plus treatment of underlying tuberculosis when present. Theodore disease follows a remitting-relapsing course over several years and typically resolves within 5-10 years, especially when associated thyroid disease is controlled. Sicca disease management runs lifelong; severity tracks the underlying systemic condition.
recoveryTimeline: EKC: symptoms peak at days 5-7 and resolve over 2-3 weeks, with subepithelial infiltrates persisting up to 6 months. VKC: seasonal flares resolve in 4-8 weeks with treatment; complete resolution by puberty in over 95%. HSV epithelial keratitis: dendrite heals in 7-14 days on antiviral therapy; stromal disease takes 2-6 months. AKC: chronic with quarterly to monthly flares; significant improvement within 12 weeks of starting dupilumab in skin-driven cases.
complications: Persistent subepithelial corneal infiltrates after EKC reducing best-corrected visual acuity for up to 12 months
Shield ulcer in vernal disease — a sterile corneal ulcer overlying severe papillary hypertrophy that can scar centrally
Steroid-induced cataract and glaucoma from prolonged topical steroid use in VKC and AKC
Keratoconus and corneal scarring in atopic patients who chronically rub their eyes
Stromal scarring and corneal neovascularization from recurrent HSV keratitis, a leading cause of corneal transplant
Secondary bacterial keratitis on a compromised viral or allergic corneal surface, particularly in contact lens wearers
Symblepharon (lid-to-globe adhesions) following severe EKC with pseudomembranes or chemical-burn-like sicca disease

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — EyeSmart
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that publishes the Preferred Practice Patterns for corneal disease.

supportResources.name: Royal National Institute of Blind People (RNIB) — Eye Conditions
supportResources.description: UK patient-facing resource covering eye conditions including conjunctivitis and keratitis.

supportResources.name: National Eye Institute — Conjunctivitis and Corneal Disease
supportResources.description: NIH patient information on conjunctivitis, dry eye, herpetic keratitis, and related conditions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis (Hay Fever)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic rhinitis primarily affects the nasal passages with sneezing, rhinorrhea, and congestion; ocular itching can occur but the cornea is spared. Vernal keratoconjunctivitis affects the eyes primarily, with cobblestone papillae, Horner-Trantas dots, and potential corneal shield ulcers — features absent in isolated rhinitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpes Simplex Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mucocutaneous HSV produces vesicular lesions on lips or genitals without ocular involvement in most cases. Herpetic keratoconjunctivitis is the ocular form, with a dendritic corneal ulcer staining with fluorescein and reduced corneal sensation — findings absent in oral or genital HSV alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sjogren syndrome is a systemic autoimmune disease producing dry eyes, dry mouth, and often joint or systemic symptoms with positive SSA/SSB antibodies. Sicca keratoconjunctivitis is the ocular manifestation; isolated dry eye without systemic features does not equal Sjogren and requires antibody testing to confirm.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic itchy skin condition with flexural eczema; the eye surface itself is not always involved. Atopic keratoconjunctivitis is the ocular extension, producing chronic eyelid eczema, conjunctival fibrosis, and corneal complications including keratoconus.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis (Hay Fever)
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares IgE/Th2-mediated allergic pathway with vernal and atopic keratoconjunctivitis; co-management addresses both surfaces.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
relatedConditions.relevance: Underlying systemic disease in atopic keratoconjunctivitis; dupilumab and topical immunomodulators improve both skin and ocular surface.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes Simplex Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: HSV-1 reactivation along the trigeminal ophthalmic branch causes herpetic keratoconjunctivitis; oral antiviral suppression reduces both oral and ocular recurrence.

relatedConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune lacrimal gland destruction in Sjogren produces severe sicca keratoconjunctivitis with corneal staining and rapid tear break-up.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Latent or active tuberculosis is a recognized driver of phlyctenular keratoconjunctivitis through delayed hypersensitivity to tuberculoprotein.

faqs.question: Is keratoconjunctivitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Some subtypes are contagious; others are not. Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis caused by adenovirus is highly contagious, spreading via hands, towels, and surfaces for up to 14 days. Allergic, atopic, sicca, and Theodore subtypes are not contagious.

faqs.question: How long does keratoconjunctivitis last?
faqs.answer: Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis lasts 2-3 weeks for active symptoms, with corneal infiltrates persisting up to 6 months. Vernal flares last 4-8 weeks and resolve by puberty. Herpetic ulcers heal in 7-14 days with antivirals. Atopic and sicca forms are chronic.

faqs.question: What is the difference between conjunctivitis and keratoconjunctivitis?
faqs.answer: Conjunctivitis is inflammation of the conjunctiva alone, the thin membrane covering the white of the eye. Keratoconjunctivitis involves both the conjunctiva and the cornea, the clear window at the front of the eye. Corneal involvement raises the stakes because it can scar and reduce vision.

faqs.question: Can keratoconjunctivitis cause permanent vision loss?
faqs.answer: Most subtypes do not cause permanent vision loss when diagnosed and treated correctly. Risk is highest with recurrent herpetic keratitis (scarring), severe vernal disease with shield ulcers, untreated bacterial superinfection, and end-stage atopic disease with corneal scarring or keratoconus.

faqs.question: How is epidemic keratoconjunctivitis different from regular pinkeye?
faqs.answer: EKC is severe viral conjunctivitis caused by adenovirus serotypes 8, 19, and 37 that produces corneal subepithelial infiltrates 7-14 days into illness. Regular viral pinkeye causes only redness, watering, and a swollen pre-auricular node without corneal involvement.

faqs.question: What causes vernal keratoconjunctivitis?
faqs.answer: Vernal keratoconjunctivitis is an allergic disease driven by IgE and Th2 cytokines in genetically predisposed children, mostly boys aged 5-15 in hot dry climates. Triggers include pollen, dust, and animal dander. Spring and summer flares give it the name vernal.

faqs.question: What are Horner-Trantas dots?
faqs.answer: Horner-Trantas dots are small white-yellow gelatinous nodules at the limbus, the junction between the cornea and conjunctiva. They are clusters of eosinophils and epithelial cells seen in limbal vernal keratoconjunctivitis. Their presence on slit-lamp examination is essentially diagnostic of VKC.

faqs.question: How is herpes keratoconjunctivitis treated?
faqs.answer: Herpes simplex epithelial keratoconjunctivitis is treated with topical trifluridine 1% nine times daily for 10-14 days or oral acyclovir 400 mg five times daily for 7-10 days. Chronic oral acyclovir 400 mg twice daily for one year halves recurrence rates per the HEDS trial.

faqs.question: Can I wear contact lenses with keratoconjunctivitis?
faqs.answer: No. Stop wearing contact lenses at the first sign of redness, pain, or discharge. Lenses worsen surface inflammation, harbor microbes, and substantially raise the risk of secondary bacterial or fungal keratitis. Discard the lens and case in use at symptom onset.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for a red eye?
faqs.answer: See an ophthalmologist within 24 hours for any red eye with reduced vision, severe pain, photophobia, foreign-body sensation over 48 hours, suspected dendritic ulcer, contact lens wear, or eye trauma. Simple watery pinkeye improving over 5-7 days is managed by primary care.

faqs.question: Are steroid eye drops safe?
faqs.answer: Topical steroid drops control inflammation but carry real risks: cataract, raised eye pressure leading to glaucoma, delayed healing, and worsening of undiagnosed herpetic disease. They require ophthalmology supervision with regular pressure checks, for the shortest effective course.

faqs.question: Will my child outgrow vernal keratoconjunctivitis?
faqs.answer: Yes, over 95% of children with vernal keratoconjunctivitis outgrow it by their early twenties. Severe cases can evolve into adult atopic keratoconjunctivitis, particularly with persistent atopic dermatitis. Disease activity peaks in spring and summer and fades after puberty.

faqs.question: How can I prevent spreading EKC to my family?
faqs.answer: Wash hands often, use separate towels and pillowcases, avoid touching the eyes, do not share cosmetics or eye drops, and stay home from work or school for 10-14 days until tearing and redness have settled. Disinfect doorknobs, phones, and remote controls. Replace all eye cosmetics after recovery.

faqs.question: Does dry eye count as keratoconjunctivitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dry eye disease severe enough to produce punctate corneal staining is formally called keratoconjunctivitis sicca, one of the seven subtypes. Most mild dry eye is conjunctivitis sicca without corneal involvement; once the cornea stains, it qualifies as keratoconjunctivitis.

faqs.question: What is the role of cyclosporine eye drops?
faqs.answer: Topical cyclosporine 0.05-2% is a calcineurin inhibitor that reduces T-cell-mediated inflammation. It is steroid-sparing maintenance for moderate-to-severe vernal and atopic keratoconjunctivitis and chronic dry eye. Onset is gradual over 4-12 weeks. Mild stinging is the main side effect.

faqs.question: Can dupilumab help atopic keratoconjunctivitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dupilumab is an IL-4/IL-13 receptor blocker approved for moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis. It improves atopic keratoconjunctivitis in most patients whose eye disease tracks their skin disease. A subset develop dupilumab-associated conjunctivitis needing joint dermatology and eye care.

faqs.question: How does Theodore superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis present?
faqs.answer: Theodore disease presents in middle-aged women with recurrent burning, foreign-body sensation, and photophobia worse with blinking. Slit-lamp shows redness and rose-bengal staining of the superior bulbar conjunctiva and limbus. About 30% of patients have associated thyroid dysfunction.

faqs.question: Does keratoconjunctivitis affect both eyes?
faqs.answer: Allergic, atopic, and sicca keratoconjunctivitis are typically bilateral. Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis often starts in one eye and spreads to the other within 2-7 days. Herpetic disease is usually unilateral. Theodore disease classically affects both superior limbi but may be asymmetric.

faqs.question: What does keratoconjunctivitis treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Most treatments are inexpensive generics: olopatadine, trifluridine, oral acyclovir, and preservative-free artificial tears cost USD 10-50 per month. Topical cyclosporine and lifitegrast run USD 200-600 monthly without insurance. Dupilumab is expensive but usually covered for atopic dermatitis.

faqs.question: Can keratoconjunctivitis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes, depending on subtype. Vernal disease flares each spring until puberty. Atopic disease flares with skin disease through adult life. Herpetic disease recurs in 25% at one year and 50% at ten years without suppression. EKC rarely recurs because immunity follows infection.

metaDescription: Keratoconjunctivitis: inflammation of cornea and conjunctiva. Viral, allergic, atopic, herpetic, and sicca subtypes with diagnosis and treatment by aihealz.
